questions.question: Why is `move` needed when spawning a thread with a closure?
options: It transfers ownership of captured variables to the thread
questions.explanation: The spawned thread may outlive the current scope. `move` transfers ownership so references stay valid.

questions.question: Rust prevents data races at compile time through ownership and the type system.
questions.explanation: Send and Sync traits, combined with ownership rules, prevent data races. Non-thread-safe types won't compile in concurrent contexts.

questions.question: Create a channel for message passing:
questions.explanation: `mpsc::channel()` creates a multi-producer, single-consumer channel. tx sends, rx receives.

questions.question: What does Mutex<T> provide?
options: Mutual exclusion — only one thread accesses data at a time
questions.explanation: `.lock()` returns a MutexGuard providing exclusive access. The lock is released when the guard drops.

questions.question: Share a Mutex across threads using Arc:
questions.explanation: Arc::clone increments the atomic reference count. Each thread gets its own Arc handle.

questions.question: The Send trait marks types whose ownership can safely transfer between threads.
questions.explanation: Almost all types are Send. Rc<T> is a notable exception — use Arc<T> instead.
